coredata.dc:title: Enhancement of synergistic effect photocatalytic/persulfate activation for degradation of antibiotics by the combination of photo-induced electrons and carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Weilong
Hao, Chenchen
Fu, Yongming
Guo, Feng
Tang, Yubin
Yan, Xu
coredata.dc:description: Photocatalytic/persulfate-oxidation hybrid (PPSOH) system is considered as a feasible method for antibiotic degradation owing to it contains affluent active species. Herein, the flower-shaped ZnIn2S4 (ZIS) nanosheets modified by carbon dots (CDs) to form CDs-ZIS composite was selected as a catalyst for degradation of tetracycline (TC) in PPOH system. The degradation experimental results reveal that the optimal photocatalytic performance of 1% Vis/CDs-ZIS/persulfate (PS) system was achieved with TC (50 mg/L) removal rate of 83% within 120 min, which is attributed to the successful introduction of CDs that can effectively combine photo-induced electrons of ZIS to co-activate PS for enhancing photocatalytic degradation of TC. The active species capturing experiments exhibit that with the unique electron transfer property of CDs, the synergistic effect between photocatalytic and persulfate-oxidation was realized, in which persulfate was captured by electrons and generated plentiful sulfate radicals (SO4•-) and hydroxyl radicals (•OH). This work provides a new idea and strategy for CDs-based composite photocatalysts for the combination of photocatalysis and advanced oxidation.

head.ce:title: Enhancement of synergistic effect photocatalytic/persulfate activation for degradation of antibiotics by the combination of photo-induced electrons and carbon dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photocatalytic/persulfate-oxidation hybrid (PPSOH) system is considered as a feasible method for antibiotic degradation owing to it contains affluent active species. Herein, the flower-shaped ZnIn2S4 (ZIS) nanosheets modified by carbon dots (CDs) to form CDs-ZIS composite was selected as a catalyst for degradation of tetracycline (TC) in PPOH system. The degradation experimental results reveal that the optimal photocatalytic performance of 1% Vis/CDs-ZIS/persulfate (PS) system was achieved with TC (50 mg/L) removal rate of 83% within 120 min, which is attributed to the successful introduction of CDs that can effectively combine photo-induced electrons of ZIS to co-activate PS for enhancing photocatalytic degradation of TC. The active species capturing experiments exhibit that with the unique electron transfer property of CDs, the synergistic effect between photocatalytic and persulfate-oxidation was realized, in which persulfate was captured by electrons and generated plentiful sulfate radicals (SO4•-) and hydroxyl radicals (•OH). This work provides a new idea and strategy for CDs-based composite photocatalysts for the combination of photocatalysis and advanced oxidation.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the transfer of antibiotic resistance genes caused by antibiotic abuse has attracted the attention of scholars about antibiotic pollution in the water environment [1–6]. Residual antibiotics in the water environment are ecologically toxic, difficult to degrade, and can easily induce the development of antibiotic resistance genes, posing a potential threat to the ecological environment and human health [7–10]. Hence, developing a cost-effective method to remove antibiotics in water is an urgent problem. Nowadays, great efforts have been devoted to exploring antibiotic wastewater treatment, such as biological treatment [11], membrane processes [12] and adsorption [13], but which have the problems of high energy consumption, low removal efficiency and incomplete degradation [14].

By contrast, photocatalytic/persulfate-oxidation hybrid (PPSOH) system is considered as the promising antibiotic treatment technology due to the advantage of possess abundant high-activity oxide species [15–18]. In addition, some studies have shown that PS can capture photogenerated electrons through self-activation to generate sulfate radicals and hydroxyl radicals. Meanwhile, the consumption of electrons caused by the PS can suppress the recombination of photo-generated carriers, which realizes the efficient synergistic degradation of pollutants by photocatalysis and PS oxidation [19,20]. Very recently, carbon materials have attracted much attention as new activators of PS oxidation, including biochar, activated carbon, nanocarbon materials (graphene, carbon nanotubes, etc.) [21]. It has been shown that combining carbonaceous materials with PS system can show obvious synergistic effects in the process of degradation, wherein carbon material, as an activator of electron transmitters, can effectively activate PS and produce a large number of sulfate free radicals on the surface of carbon particles, to achieve the purpose of degrading organic pollutants[22]. Although carbon materials activated PS provides a valuable platform in the field of advanced oxidation, recent studies of activated PS based on carbon materials have focused on modification of carbon materials, while studies combining activated PS technology with other technologies for synergistic degradation of contaminants are less reported. In order to realize the combination of carbon material activated PS technology and other oxidation technologies, it is of important significance to design a semiconductor photocatalyst containing a novel nanocarbon materials (carbon dots, CDs) for enhancing the ability to degrade antibiotic pollutants in the PPSOH system. For example, we initially designed a CDs/ZnIn2S4 composite photocatalyst to explain our idea.

ZnIn2S4, a ternary chalcogenide, is regarded as a promising material in the field of photocatalysis owing to its excellent visible light absorption capacity and appropriate band gap (∼2.4 eV) [23]. Unfortunately, ZnIn2S4 has the common disadvantage of most semiconductors: the short life of photo-generated charge carriers, which limits its practical application [24]. Carbon dots (CDs), as a novel carbon nanomaterials, possesses outstanding potential for catalytic fields due to the following advantages, such as excellent optical properties, abundant surface functional groups, low cost, low toxicity, environmental friendliness and simple synthesis [25–28]. In addition, CDs can enhance the visible light response of semiconductor-based photocatalysts and improve the separation of photoexcited electrons and holes in photocatalytic system owing to its excellent photo-generated electron transfer properties [26]. Previous studies have demonstrated that the introduction of CDs can effectively enhance the photocatalytic activity of ZnIn2S4. For instance, Liu et al. synthesized ZnIn2S4/carbon quantum dots hybrid photocatalysts for photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI), and the results showed that the photocatalytic activity of ZnIn2S4/carbon quantum dots was slightly improved [29]. Obviously, the introduction of CDs has a limited effect on improving the activity of photocatalytic system. In order to make full use of the excellent photo-induced electron transfer characteristics of CDs, we consider combining photocatalysis with PS oxidation in the CDs-ZnIn2S4 composite system.

Herein, the flower-shaped CDs-ZnIn2S4 composite was prepared by simple and efficient solvothermal method, which cooperated with PS to degrade tetracycline (TC) under visible light irradiation. This is due to TC antibiotics and their residues that have been detected in the water environment, posing a potential threat to the ecological environment and human health. This work exhibits the following two contributions in the field of photocatalysis: on the one hand, the visible light response and the separation efficiency of photoinduced electron-hole pairs of the composites were improved by the introduction of CDs in the CDs-ZnIn2S4 composite; on the other hand, due to the unique properties of electron transmission “bridge” and electron “reservoir”, CDs effectively promote PS to capture electrons from conduction band, thus maximizing the synergistic effect between photocatalysis and PS oxidation. In addition, the species and relative number of active species in Vis/CDs-ZnIn2S4/PS system were verified by free radical trapping experiment and electron spin resonance (ESR) test. Based on the optical properties and liquid chromatography mass spectrometry (LC-MS) analysis, the possible reaction mechanism and degradation path of the system were proposed and discussed.

All chemicals were in analytic purity and all solutions were prepared in ultrapure water. The TC, InCl3·4H2O, fulvic acids (FA) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. ZnCl2, thioacetamide (TAA), NaOH, H2SO4, Na2S2O8, Na2CO3, NaCl, NaNO3, K2SO4, MnSO4·H2O, CuSO4·5H2O, FeSO4·7H2O, Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, methanol (CH3OH, >99.5%) (MeOH), tert-butanol (TBA), vitamin C (VC) and disodium ethylene diamine tetra-acetic acid (EDTA) were provided by Sino-pharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs, ZnIn2S4 and CDs-ZnIn2S4 composites
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs solution was prepared through the reported electrochemical method [30], and the CDs solution prepared by the above method is freeze-dried into a black powder for subsequent use. The CDs-ZnIn2S4 composites were prepared by a solvothermal method (Scheme 1 ). Typically, the Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O (0.2 mmol), InCl3·4H2O (0.4 mmol) and TAA (0.8 mmol) were added into the mixed solution composed of 30 mL ultrapure water and 30 mL absolute ethanol, stirred at room temperature for 30 min. Then, the as-synthesized CDs powder with various mass ratios were put into the above solution with ultrasonic treatment for 1 h. Subsequently, the solution was then transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined steel autoclave and hydrothermally treated at 180 °C for 24 h. Finally, the products were collected by centrifugation and further washed several times alternately with deionized water and absolute ethanol, and dried to obtain cyan powder at vacuum environment. Because the mass ratios of CDs in CDs-ZIS composites were 0.3, 1, 2 and 3 wt%, the as-synthesized photocatalysts were denoted as 0.3% CDs-ZIS, 1% CDs-ZIS, 2% CDs-ZIS and 3% CDs-ZIS, respectively. In addition, pristine ZnIn2S4 was prepared by the same method without adding CDs and named as ZIS.
The detail characterizations, photodegradation process, active species trapping experiments and degradation product analysis were listed in Supporting Information .

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of ZIS and 1% CDs-ZIS were shown in Fig. 1 a. The characteristic peaks of ZIS at 2θ = 15.5°, 21.6°, 27.7°, 30.4°, 39.9°, 47.2°, 52.4° and 55.6° were corresponded to (002), (006), (102), (104), (108), (110), (116) and (022) lattice planes of hexagonal ZnIn2S4 (JCPDS 72–0773), respectively [31]. Fig. S1 shown the XRD pattern of single CDs, the broad peak located at around 26° can be observed, which is ascribed to the graphic carbon [32]. However, no obvious diffraction peaks of CDs were observed in the composite, which may be owing to the low quantity of CDs [33]. Fig. 1b displays the FT-IR spectra of CDs and 1% CDs-ZIS composite. The peak of CDs and 1% CDs-ZIS at 1716 cm−1 and 3430 cm−1 are ascribed to the O–H bending and stretching vibration of H2O molecules absorbed to the surface of sample [34]. The peak of CDs at 1241, 1403 and 1631 cm−1 are related to C-OH, C = C and C = O bonds, respectively [30]. In addition, those peaks can be observed from the FT-IR spectra of 1% CDs-ZIS, which strongly verified that CDs were successfully introduced into the surface of ZIS nanosheets. The Raman spectra of CDs, ZIS and 1% CDs-ZIS were also measured and shown in Fig. S2. For pure CDs, two prominent peaks at 1326 and 1602 cm−1 can be clearly observed and corresponded to G band and D band of carbon, respectively [35]. Meanwhile, the two characteristic peaks of carbon can be observed in 1% CDs-ZIS composite, which further proved the existence of CDs.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscope (TEM) are carried out to reveal the morphologies of the CDs, ZIS and 1% CDs-ZIS. The TEM and HRTEM image of CDs was shown in Fig. S3, it can be seen that the prepared carbon dots show uniformly dispersed quasi-spherical morphology, with the average diameter of about 5 nm. In addition, the lattice spacing of CDs is 0.21 nm, which coincides with the (100) lattice plane of graphite carbon [36]. In Fig. 2 a and b, it can be clearly observed that pure ZIS is a flower-like structure assembled by numerous nanosheets. Fig. S4 exhibits the TEM image of 1% CDs-ZIS, the flower-like structure of ZIS was not destroyed after the introduction of CDs. However, only ZIS nanosheets can be seen in the low-resolution TEM image of 1%CD-ZIS (Fig. 2c), and no presence of CDs was observed. Therefore, the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) was further employed to observe whether the lattice stripes of CDs exist or not. As exhibited in Fig. 2d, the lattice fringe spacing of 0.32 nm and 0.21 nm in 1% CDs-ZIS belong to ZIS (102) and CDs (100), respectively [23]. In addition, the high-angle annular dark-field scanning trans-mission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM) and the corresponding energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) element mapping image of 1% CDs-ZIS sample (Fig. 2e) were also performed, and Zn, In, S and C elements were clearly observed in 1% CDs-ZIS photocatalyst. These results confirmed the successful synthesis of CDs-ZIS composite photocatalysts [37].

The optical absorption property of as-synthesized ZIS and CDs-ZIS composite was measured by DRS and displayed in Fig. 3 a. The pure ZIS shows an absorption edge at 500 nm, and the corresponding estimated bandgap was 2.64 eV, which is similar to the reported literature [38]. After the loading of CDs, the light absorption range of all CDs-ZIS composite increased significantly, especially in the long wavelength range. Moreover, with the increase of CDs dosage, the light absorption capacity of the CDs-ZIS composites continuously improved, which is conducive to the generate more photo-induced charges [39]. According to the Kubelka-Munk plot, the band gap of ZIS and 1% CDs-ZIS was calculated to be around 2.64 and 2.37 eV (Fig. 3b). The Mott-Schottky plot was gained to analyze the band structure of ZIS and 1% CDs-ZIS composite in Fig. 3c, and the positive slope of Mott-Schottky curve revealed that the n-type nature of ZIS and 1% CDs-ZIS [40].In addition, it can be observed that the flat band position (Vfb) of ZIS and 1% CDs-ZIS is around −1.02 and −1.14 eV (vs. NHE, pH = 7). Due to the Vfb position of n-type semiconductor is approximately equal to the conduction band (CB) potential [41], the VCB value of ZIS and 1% CDs-ZIS is determined to be −1.02 and −1.14 eV. Combining the gained band-gap energies (Eg ) with the formula Eg = EVB - ECB
[42], the valence band (VB) potential of ZIS and 1% CDs-ZIS is estimated to be 1.64 and 1.23 eV. Depend on the results of band-gap position, the schematic diagram of the band structure of ZIS and 1% CDs-ZIS was gained and displayed on Fig. 3d, after the introduction of CDs, the light absorption capacity of 1% CDs-ZIS was enhanced than that of ZIS. The band gap of 1% CDs-ZIS is narrower than ZIS, which caused the valence band and conduction band position shifts slightly. Furthermore, it can be seen that the 1% CDs-ZIS composite has a strong potential to produce superoxide radicals (•O2 −) for the degradation of TC from the thermodynamic analysis in theory.

X-ray photoelectron spectrums (XPS) of as-prepared ZIS and 1% CDs-ZIS were examined to explore the chemical composition and chemical state. Fig. S5 displayed the XPS survey spectrum of 1% CDs-ZIS, and Zn, In, S and C were observed in 1% CDs-ZIS composite. The Zn 2p spectrum of 1% CDs-ZIS in Fig. 4 a exhibited two distinct peaks at 1022.0 and 1045.1 eV, which could be ascribed to Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2, respectively [43]. In the In 3d spectrum of 1% CDs-ZIS (Fig. 4b), there are two distinct peaks at 445.2 and 452.8 eV, corresponding to the In 3d5/2 and In 3d3/2, respectively [44]. In Fig. 4c, the S 2p spectrum of 1% CDs-ZIS can be fitted into two peaks at 162.0 and 163.1 eV, belonging to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2, respectively [45]. The elemental spectrums of Zn, In and S confirmed that their valence states are + 2, +3 and −2, respectively [46]. Interestingly, the binding energy values of In and S in 1% CDs-ZIS was higher than that of ZIS, which can be attributed to the charge transfer between CDs and ZIS [47]. Fig. 4d displayed the C 1 s spectrum of 1% CDs-ZIS sample, which can be fitted into three peaks located at 284.7, 285.4 and 289.1 eV, and those peaks were ascribed to the C–C/C = C, C-O and C = O bond, respectively. The above results confirmed the successful synthesis of 1% CDs-ZIS composite [32].

The photocatalytic performance of the as-prepared photocatalysts and its ability to activate persulfate (PS) were evaluated by degrading TC. Before illumination, the reaction solution was stirred for 1 h in the dark to achieve adsorption–desorption equilibrium. All the reaction processes follow the condition of adding PS after dark reaction, and Xenon lamp is used as light source. As illustrated in Fig. S6, after 120 min of illumination, the degradation rate of pure ZIS is 68%. In addition, after the introducing of CDs, the degradation effect of TC with CDs-ZIS composites was slightly improved, while the adsorption effect was slightly decreased, and 1% CDs-ZIS composite exhibited the most outstanding photocatalytic performance. Nevertheless, with the increase of CDs (form 1% to 3%), the photocatalytic activity of CDs-ZIS composites decreased, which may be due to excessive CDs with covering the reaction active sites on the surface of CDs-ZIS composite [48]. Obviously, the photocatalytic activity of the CDs-ZIS composites is not significantly improved after the introduction of CDs, so adding PS can make full use of the properties of electron donors/acceptors of CDs in the reaction system. The degradation curves of TC in different systems given in Fig. 5 a, and it can be seen that TC hardly degrades and can be neglected under visible light irradiation. In the existence of only PS, just 8% of TC was removed after 120 min, and in the presence of visible light (Vis) and PS, only 21% TC was degraded within 120 min, which can be attributed to light-induced photothermal effects [49]. Compared with without PS, the degradation rate of TC of ZIS increased by 5% after 120 min of illumination in the presence of PS, and the degradation rate increased significantly in the first 40 min, which confirms the PS activation of photo-excited electrons [19]. However, compared with ZIS under the activation of PS, the 1% CDs-ZIS composite photocatalyst activated PS obtained more prominent effect, and the degradation rate of TC (50 mg/L) can be reached to 83% in Vis/1% CDs-ZIS/PS system, which was attributed to the excellent photo-induced electron transfer property of CDs [50–52]. In addition, TC was degraded by the physical mixture of ZIS and CDs (1% CDs&ZIS), and it was found that the removal rate of TC decreased significantly, which further confirmed that the CDs were successfully loaded in the 1% CDs-ZIS composite in a tightly combined interface manner. Fig. S7 displayed the photocatalytic degradations curve and the pseudo-first-order reaction kinetics –ln (C/C0)-t curves of different reaction systems within 20 min, the photocatalytic degradation of TC in different reaction systems generally follows quasi-first-order kinetics. Obviously, the photocatalytic activity of the whole reaction system is obviously improved under the condition of coexistence of CDs and PS. At the same time, the mineralization of TC by different reaction system was also evaluated by detecting total organic carbon (TOC) content of solution after photocatalytic reaction for 120 min. As exhibited in Fig. S8, the Vis/1% CDs-ZIS/PS system displays the highest TOC removal rate of 63%, followed by 51%, 44% and 40% for Vis/ZIS/PS, Vis/ZIS and Vis/1% CDs-ZIS, which indicates the Vis/1% CDs-ZIS/PS system could realize both removal and mineralization of the pollutants at a very high level. Furthermore, Table S1 presented the comparison of performance of activated PS with different materials for degradation of TC. Compared with most carbon-based materials, 1% Vis/CDs-ZIS/PS possesses the higher photocatalytic activity, which is comparable to that of metal activated PS technology, and the catalyst dosage was small. In order to study the effect of 1% CDs-ZIS catalyst dose on the photocatalytic degradation of TC in Vis/1% CDs-ZIS/PS system, during the experiment, the catalyst dosage was changed from 50 to 200 mg/L. As displayed in Fig. 5b, the removal rate of TC increased significantly with the increase of catalyst loading (50 to 100 mg/L), which may be ascribed to the increase of the photocatalytic active sites and available active sites for PS decomposition [53]. Nevertheless, the degradation rate of TC did not improve further with the increase of 1% CD-ZIS catalyst dose, possibly due to the shielding effect of suspended particles caused to over-catalyst weakening the penetration of light [54]. In contrast, when the dosage of catalyst is 100 mg/L, it is most beneficial to the degradation of TC. Therefore, the 100 mg/L of catalyst dose was used to carried out all photocatalytic experiment in this study. Fig. 5c showed the effect of different PS concentration on TC degradation, when PS increases from 0.25 to 0.75 g/L, the TC degradation rate increases from 73% to 83%, but when the concentration of PS was increased, the degradation rate of TC decreased gradually, this may be because the electrons produced by a fixed amount of catalyst in the reaction system are limited, and excessive PS will react with produced sulfate radical, leading to a decrease of active species in the reaction system, thus reducing the activity of the whole reaction system [55,56]. The degradation rate of TC in Vis/1% CDs-ZIS/PS system at different TC concentrations (100–700 mg/L) was studied and shown in Fig. 5d. It can be obvious that TC at low concentration (100 and 200 mg/L) was almost completely degraded within 60 min. In addition, the degradation rate of TC decreased with the of TC concentration gradually increase. This phenomenon may be due to a large number of by-products would compete with TC molecules to consume radicals [53].

The effects of pH on TC degradation in Vis/1% CDs-ZIS/PS systems were investigated under various pH values of (3, 5, 7 and 9) and given in Fig. 6 a. It can be seen that the degradation rate of TC is not affected at neutral and acidic conditions, and the degradation rate of TC is almost 100% at pH = 9, which demonstrated that alkaline environment is more favorable for the degradation of TC. This is ascribed to the fact that alkali activated PS can produce superoxide, besides, PS can react with hydroxide to produce more hydroxyl radicals [57]. Natural organic matter (NOM) or other background components, such as cations and anions, are ubiquitous in natural water, which could affect the adsorption and oxidation of pollutants during the photocatalytic reaction [58]. Thus, the effect of anions (including Cl-, CO3 2– and NO3 –) was studied in the presence of different concentration anions (1, 5 and 10 mmol/L) and the three anions came from NaCl, Na2CO3 and NaNO3 during the photocatalysis in the 1% CDs-ZIS/PS system. Fig. 6b displayed the effect of Cl- on TC degradation, it can be observed that Cl- exhibits significant inhibiting effect on the decomposition of TC, which can be attributed to the generation of species (such as ClHO·, Cl· and Cl2 - ·) with relatively lower redox potentials [59,60]. As depicted in Fig. 6c, the degradation effect of TC was significantly improved in the presence of CO3 2–, which may be due to the fact that the solution pH values increased after the adding of CO3 2–. This is because PS tends to decompose into active free radicals under alkaline conditions, resulting in more ·OH and ·SO4 - in the reaction process [61]. In Fig. 6d, NO3 – showed a slight inhibitory effect on TC degradation, this may be owing to NO3 – almost do not react with sulfate radicals [62]. The effect of common metal cations on the degradation of TC was investigated and given in Fig. 6e. The K+ and Mn2+ showed a strong inhibitory effect on TC degradation, which may be attributed to the fact that K+ and Mn2+ ions are the ineffective activator for PS, but the K+ and Mn2+ ions have the ability to attract electrons, and the reduction of the number of electrons further reduces the yield of ·O2 – [63,64]. Cu2+, Fe2+ and Fe3+ displayed similar effects on TC degradation, possibly due to Cu2+, Fe2+ and Fe3+ can activate PS to generate more sulfate radicals [62]. Fulvic acids (FA), as a typical aquatic natural organic matter (NOM), exhibits a slight inhibition on TC degradation, and inhibitory effect increases with the increasing FA concentration (Fig. 6f). This may be due to the NOM that can influence the radical oxidation reaction by quenching the radical and convert the radical intermediates into the parent compound [58,65].

The stability of the 1% CDs-ZIS photocatalyst was confirmed through three successive cycling experiments. In Fig. 7 a, the 1% CDs-ZIS still maintained about 70 % removal rate for TC degradation after five repeated tests. At the same time, the XRD patterns of the 1% CDs-ZIS before and after the cycle experiment were displayed in Fig. 7b, and it can be seen that the peak of XRD spectrum over 1% CDs-ZIS has no obvious change, proving that its structure and composition remained unchanged even after three cycling experiments. The above results sufficiently proved the excellent stability and potential for practical application of as-prepared 1% CDs-ZIS composite in this work.

Identification of intermediates is a method to further analyze the degradation mechanism of TC and the degradation intermediates were given by L-MS (Fig. S9), and then the possible degradation pathway was proposed and shown in Fig. 8 . The functional groups containing double bonds in TC, such as phenolic and amino, are relatively high and are therefore vulnerable to active species. For the path 1, the double bond may be attacked by ·SO4 - radicals to form P1 (m/z 477) [66]. Subsequently, under the attack of free radicals, P1 was formed into P2 (m/z 434) with the loss of N-methyl and amino groups [67], then P3 (m/z 371) and P4 (m/z 256) were formed by further dehydroxylation, decarboxylation, deamidation and ring-opening [68]. For the other path 2, TC was converted into P5 (m/z 318) through dehydroxylation, deamidation, demethylation and deamination under the attack of free radicals, and then which was oxidized by ·O2 – and h+ and converted into P6 (m/z 274) and P7 (m/z 246) with the ring opening reactions [19]. Subsequently, P4 and P7 were further oxidized and gradually transformed into small molecular substances (P8a/b m/z 186), H2O, CO2 and etc.

Figure 9 a displayed the N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms of ZIS and CDs-ZIS. Compared with ZIS, the specific surface area of CDs-ZIS decreased slightly, which may be due to the introduction of CDs blocking the pores of ZIS, and which is consistent with the result of poor adsorption performance of CDs-ZIS [69–72]. The photoelectrochemical properties of the ZIS and prepared CDs-ZIS composites were discussed through photoluminescence spectra (PL), transient photocurrent responses and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). Fig. 9b illustrates the PL spectra of ZIS and prepared CDs-ZIS composites under the excitation wavelength of 325 nm. It can be seen that 1% CDs-ZIS has a lower PL emission signal than pure-phase ZIS, indicating that the introduction of CDs effectively prevents the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and further improves the photocatalytic activity of ZIS [73–76]. In order to further study the charge separation performance of ZIS and prepared CDs-ZIS composites, the transient photocurrent responses were carried out and given in Fig. 9c. Obviously, the transient photocurrent response of CDs-ZIS is significantly enhanced compared with the pure the ZIS. Meanwhile the impedance arc radius of CDs-ZIS was also smaller than ZIS in Fig. 9d, sufficiently proving the excellent charge transfer capacity and high electrical conductivity of CDs modified ZIS [77–80].

In order to study the degradation mechanism of the system combining of photocatalysis and PS activation, the trapping experiments and ESR tests were carried out to examine the main active species. Fig. 10 a displayed the degradation effect of TC in the presence of different sacrificial agents (TBA/·OH, MeOH/·SO4 -, VC/·O2 –, EDTA/h+ ) [19,81–85], it can be seen that ·OH, ·SO4 -, ·O2 – and h+ take a role in the degradation process, while h+ plays a relatively more important role. As we know, ·SO4 - and ·OH react quickly with methanol (MeOH) (k ·
SO4- = (1.6–7.7) × 105 M−1s−1, ·OH =(1.2–2.8) × 108 M−1s−1). Moreover, tertiary butanol (TBA) can react to ·OH with more fast kinetic rates (k ·
OH = (3.8–7.6) × 108 M−1s−1) than that with ·SO4 - (k ·
SO4- = (4.0–9.1) × 105 M−1s−1) [86,87]. In this work, MeOH (22.7 %) has a stronger inhibitory effect on the degradation of TC than TBA (16.5 %), indicating that ·SO4 - plays a leading role in Vis/1%-CDs-ZIS/PS system compared with ·OH. Furthermore, the presence of ·SO4 - and ·OH reveals the successful activation of PS by the photo-induced electrons stored in the CDs. To further probe the existence of active species in the reaction system, DMPO was used as radical trapping agent for ESR measurements. As exhibited in Fig. 10b-d, the signals of DMPO -·OH/·SO4 -, DMPO-·O2 – and TEMPO-h+ adducts can be observed, confirming again the existence of four radicals (·OH, ·SO4 -, ·O2 – and h+) during the reaction system.

Based on the above test results, the possible mechanism of Vis/1% CDs-ZIS/PS system for TC degradation was proposed and illustrated in Fig. 11 . When CDs-ZIS was irradiated under visible light with the presence of PS, three main processes will take place: (i) the ZIS produces photogenerated electrons and holes under the excitation of visible light (Eq. (1)). Due to the electron transfer characteristics of CDs, the photogenerated electrons are transferred to the surface of CDs, which can activate the PS adsorbed on the material surface and produce ·SO4 -(Eq. (2)), which further reacts with H2O/OH– to generate ·OH (Eq. (3))[88]; (ii) at the same time, the free electrons of CB are captured by O2 in aqueous solution to generate ·O2 – (Eq. (4)) [89]; (iii) in addition, the h+ in the VB of ZIS have strong oxidizability, and these above active species and h+ can oxidize TC into small organic molecules, H2O and CO2 (Eq. (5)) [56,90,91]. Compared with the normal photocatalytic reaction, in the process of PS-mediated photocatalytic reaction, the CB electrons are captured by O2, some by PS, and only a small part was recombined with holes, the trapping of CB electrons by PS can further effectively promote the separation and migration of photogenerated electrons and holes, thus improving the quantum efficiency of photocatalysis. The detailed active species can be generated in the Vis/CDs-ZIS/PS system from the following route:

In this study, the CDs-ZIS composite photocatalyst was prepared by a simple solvothermal method, and a series of characterization proved that the successful introduction of CDs improved the light absorption and the separation and migration efficiency of photogenerated electrons and holes of ZIS. In addition, PS was introduced into the CDs-ZIS photocatalytic system, which successfully realized the combination of advanced oxidation and photocatalysis, and made full use of the electron transmission performance of CDs, thus, the optimal 1% CDs-ZIS has achieved excellent activities in degrading high concentration TC solution (83% within 120 min). The experimental results are also able to confirm that CDs can effectively combine photo-induced electrons for co-activating PS to produce more active species (·OH, ·SO4 -, ·O2 – and h+ ) in the reaction system. This work provides a new idea and strategy for a better combination of photocatalysis and advanced oxidation.
